data.title: Automatic Data Processing: Still Expensive Given Macro Headwinds
data.description:
Shares of Automatic Data Processing, Inc. (ADP) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 15% of their value and missing out on a market rally. The combination of a slowing jobs market and lower short-term rates have weighed on shares, especially as its expensive valuation left little margin for error. I last covered ADP in June 2024 when I rated the stock a Strong Sell, and since then, its 3% returned has significantly lagged the market’s 29% gain. Still, with a positive return, my rating was likely too harsh and a Sell rating would have been appropriate given the relative underperformance.

With updated financials and a new macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit ADP.

In the company’s second quarter, ADP earned $2.62, which beat consensus by $0.05 as revenue increased by 6% to $5.4 billion. EBIT increased by 10% to $1.4 billion thanks to revenue growth and 80bps of margin expansion. Thus far, the company is managing through macro headwinds relatively well, especially as fixed reinvestment of its cash portfolio helps to offset Fed rate cuts. Client wins are also helping to support revenue even as jobs growth slows.

Employment growth may slow results going forward

In its Employer Services segment, revenue grew by 6% to $3.6 billion, aided ~1% by currency movement. Pays per control was up 1%; this metric essentially matters same-client employee growth, and it is consistent with slow but positive growth in the jobs market. Thanks to stronger interest income and cost efficiencies, margins expanded by 50bps to 35.4%. For fiscal 2026, the company continues to expect a 4-7% revenue benefit from new bookings, partially offset by 20bps of attrition. ADP also expects pays per control to be flattish, pointing to modest deceleration in the jobs market.

As you can see below, the pace of employment growth has been slowing for several years. Much of this reflects the natural aging of the economic cycle (potential jobs growth is slower when unemployment is lower); Fed rate hikes also intentionally slowed labor market momentum. Recent cuts have been aimed at stabilizing employment more so than accelerating job growth, but we have actually seen the pace of slowing worsen over the past six months.

Demographics are also likely posing a challenge. Population growth in the US slowed to just 0.5% last year, one of the lowest levels on records. Immigration levels were essentially halved to 1.3 million (note this metric does not distinguish between legal and illegal immigration), given the Administration’s more aggressive enforcement of immigration law. If anything, I expect a slower pace in 2026. Slower population growth means, all else equal, slower potential employment growth.

Continued slowing in the labor market will be a headwind for ADP’s business, and considering all of these factors, I struggle to see a reacceleration in hiring. There is a very real possibility that employment growth could turn negative in 2026, though this is not my base case. Historically, negative employment growth almost always coincides with a recession, and I would expect more forceful Fed action if this occurred. I also expect the health of consumer balance sheets to support modest economic growth, which should keep employment roughly stable. Still, pays per control is likely to continue slowing in coming quarters.

Elsewhere, its PEO services unit grew revenue by 6% to $1.76 billion. However, this included a 3% boost in zero-margin pass-through costs. Because of this mix shift, margins fell by 70bps to 14.4%. Profitable revenue grew a more modest 3% with its employee count up 2% to 760,000. This business functions like the HR division of small business who outsource employment and benefits to ADP. Given the weakening labor market, I expect ongoing deceleration here, and given the fact most small businesses have lower margins, I am more cautious on employment trends here than at larger firms.

Fixed reinvestment offsets rate cuts

Finally, interest on funds held for clients increased by $36 million to $309 million. Balances were up 6% to $37.6 billion, and the interest earned on funds rose to 3.3% from 3.1%. This increase in yield came despite lower cash yields, given Fed rate cuts. This was offset by a 70bp increase in yields in its extended client balances, where it holds maturities up to 5-years to reduce the portfolio’s rate sensitivity. As post-COVID purchases roll off this portfolio, it can reinvest proceeds at higher yield. I see another 15-20bps of potential yield increase thanks to fixed reinvestment, and I expect ADP’s portfolio to reach its peak yield by the end of 2026.

Cash flow supports capital returns

ADP has an excellent balance sheet with $2.5 billion of cash and equivalents and just $4 billion of long-term debt. The company has consistently maintained very low debt levels, using its strong balance sheet as a competitive advantage, given it holds customer cash to process payroll and pay employees. Given this, I view it unlikely ADP would lever up its balance sheet meaningfully to return capital to investors, though it can use all of its free cash flow for capital returns.

ADP has generated about $1.7 billion of free cash flow year to date, despite over $1.3 billion of working capital headwinds (consistent with the company’s seasonal patterns). Earlier this month, the company authorized a $6 billion buyback authorization, which will likely take multiple years to be completed. So far this fiscal year, ADP has bought back $882 million of stock. Thanks to these buybacks, its share count is down about 1% from a year ago. Beyond this, shares offer a 2.7% dividend yield.

Shares remain expensive

Alongside results, the company raised its outlook for the fiscal year. With half of the year complete, it now expects revenue to be at the high end of guidance with EPS in the upper half of the range. This update is primarily thanks to a weaker dollar and slightly higher client funds. With half of the year complete, guidance should be fairly accurate, especially as employment trends typically move gradually. I also do not expect any rate cuts before April (I assume 2 in 2026), which provides clear visibility for cash revenue.

I am looking for the company to earn abut $10.95 in fiscal 2026 and about $11.25 over the next 12 months given my assumptions of 2 calendar year rate cuts and 0-0.5% employment growth. This leaves shares at about 22x earnings. When I last covered ADP 18 months ago, shares were closer to 26x, so as the company has steadily grown and shares have traded sideways, we have seen its multiple compress to more normal levels. Indeed, I view its excessive valuation, more so than operating results, as being the primary challenge for shares’ performance.

While valuation is not extreme, 22x for a company with medium-term mid single-digit earnings growth is expensive given the economy is near full employment and short-term rates are likely heading lower over time. ADP has an excellent balance sheet, which can support valuation. However, this strength is structural in nature, and a $5-10 billion debt-funded buyback is highly unlikely. I view fair value as closer to 20x, or $225. The fact shares are 2% lower in early trading despite the beat and raise speaks to how elevated valuation remains. With about 10% downside, I view ADP as a Sell and would continue to avoid the stock. Underperformance is likely to continue.

data.title: Reserve Bank of Australia On Track For A February Rate Hike
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava, Regional Head of Research, Asia-Pacific

CPI inflation for December - and therefore for the fourth quarter of 2025 - surprised to the upside. Trimmed mean CPI rose to 3.4% year-on-year, slightly above the 3.3% consensus, driven by persistent price pressures in housing (up 5.5% YoY) and in recreation and culture (up 4.5% YoY). This marks a continuation of the trend seen through the third and fourth quarters, where services inflation has remained elevated and increasingly structural, rather than transitory, keeping inflation well above the RBA’s 2-3% target range.

This outcome challenges the RBA’s previous assessment. In November, the Bank suggested that part of the September‑quarter inflation increase was likely temporary and expected quarterly inflation to moderate in the December quarter. The latest figures clearly run counter to that expectation. With inflation now looking both broad-based and persistent, it will be difficult for the RBA to attribute the rise to temporary, supply‑driven factors or to electricity rebates.

Labour market data reinforces the picture of underlying resilience – though not one of overheating. Total employment growth in December 2025 rose to an eight‑month high, with more than 80% of the increase coming from full‑time positions. However, the three‑month trend still shows that underlying momentum is being driven mainly by part‑time employment. This distinction matters: part‑time jobs - typically lower paid and offering less security, fewer benefits, and more volatile income - tend to support household spending less than gains in full‑time employment.

Other labour indicators also point to firmness. The unemployment rate fell 20bp to 4.1% in December, bringing the fourth-quarter average to 4.25%, below the RBA’s projection of 4.4%. That said, labour data during the December–January period tends to be volatile; a similar pattern occurred in early 2025, when a sharp 25k rise in full‑time jobs in January was followed by several months of softer growth.

Taken together, these developments strengthen the case for further tightening. We now expect the RBA to raise the cash rate by 25bp in February, as the December CPI print confirms inflation remains consistently above target and is unlikely to moderate in line with the Bank’s earlier expectations. At the same time, GDP growth of 2.1% in the third quarter is far from overheating, and recent high‑frequency indicators have been mixed. This suggests that if the RBA does proceed with a February hike, it is likely to be a cautious one, reflecting the delicate balance between supporting growth and containing stubbornly high inflation.

data.title: BNY Mellon Appreciation Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
“ Governments across the world are rewriting economic and foreign policies and transforming trade relationships that have anchored stability in the preceding decades. These changes have resulted in unprecedented uncertainty and volatility.”

Market Review

The S&P 500 Index gained 2.66% in a volatile quarter, as better-than- expected corporate earnings propelled the benchmark to record highs despite several growing risks. The quarter’s strong earnings season painted a picture of a resilient economy, but concerns of a potential artificial intelligence (AI) bubble and a more cautious consumer dampened the outlook. Central bank policy continues to be accommodative while trade tensions cooled in the quarter, but both themes face uncertainty in the year ahead.

Volatility was heightened in the quarter with investors scrutinizing key performance themes, including AI investments and consumer resiliency. Many large technology companies reported strong revenue and earnings growth during the period and pledged to increase capital expenditures, as tremendous computing demand outstrips current supply. As a corollary to earnings results, a series of partnerships between OpenAI and public technology companies were announced throughout the quarter that totaled over $1 trillion in future spending on AI chips, datacenters, and cloud computing. Investor concerns arose around the circular nature of funding deals, which seemed reminiscent of vendor financing, increased debt burdens, and the uncertain return profile of these investments. Notably, strong earnings from bellwether NVIDIA resulted in a muted market response, suggesting that investors became more discerning of AI-related investments this quarter.

Outside of information technology, the industrials sector also benefited from continued data center construction and investments made to modernize the electric grid. Within the financials sector, major banks saw earnings growth driven by strong loan and advisory activity, while some information services providers came under pressure from perceived AI threats to their business. Retail-facing companies reported higher levels of consumer uncertainty as high interest rates delay large purchase decisions, such as home renovation projects. Consumer reports highlighted an increasingly pronounced bifurcation, with higher-income consumers continuing to spend broadly and lower-income consumers seeking out value and trading down. Companies have largely been able to absorb tariff costs to-date, but management teams warned that they will have to raise prices eventually to make up for increased expenses.

Performance Summary

The BNY Mellon Appreciation Fund (DGAGX) underperformed the S&P 500 Index in the fourth quarter of 2025.

Market Review (continued)

The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) must navigate the tension between its dual mandate of taming inflation and supporting full employment with only one policy tool, which remains a challenge given stubborn inflation and rising unemployment. In the quarter, U.S. inflation remained above 2%; however, the low-hiring job market and unemployment rate uptick to 4.6% prompted the Fed to cut the Federal Funds rate by 25 basis points both in October and December. The Fed believes the current Federal Funds rate range of 3.50-3.75% is relatively neutral and neither supportive nor a hindrance to growth, signaling a potential pause before further easing is warranted. Investors anticipate that a new Fed Chairman may replace Chairman Powell at the end of his term in May, which could amplify the dissenting views within the Fed.

On trade policy, President Trump and Chinese Leader Xi met for their long-awaited meeting in South Korea and agreed on a series of de-escalatory moves that reversed trade restrictions previously imposed in a tit-for-tat manner leading up to the meeting. In a further thawing of relations, the U.S. government approved the sale of scaled-down AI chips to China. The oscillating nature of tariff negotiations and a looming U.S. Supreme Court decision remain a risk.

Investors paid greater attention to the multitude of risks this quarter, including a potential AI bubble, deteriorating employment data, and prolonged uncertainty over tariffs. Despite these challenges, strong corporate earnings drove gains during the period

Performance Review

The Fund trailed the Index in the period, driven by an overall negative stock selection effect. The Fund benefited from a positive selection effect within the consumer discretionary and communication services sectors, resulting in positive relative results. Within the consumer staples sector, a favorable underweight allocation and positive stock selection effect combined to support relative results.

Conversely, the Fund’s disadvantageous stock selection across the industrials, information technology, and financials sectors detracted from relative performance.

Top Contributors

The top contributors to relative performance include Alphabet, Intuitive Surgical, LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton (LVMHF), Apple, and Eli Lilly (LLY).

Top Detractors

The top detractors from relative performance include Microsoft, Meta Platforms, ServiceNow (NOW), BAE Systems (BAESY), and Eaton (ETN).

Market Outlook

The Fund’s investment philosophy remains consistent during periods of political and economic transformation. Governments across the world are rewriting economic and foreign policies and transforming trade relationships that have anchored stability in the preceding decades. These changes have resulted in unprecedented uncertainty and volatility. Harking back on our decades of investment experience, especially during uncertain or volatile markets, we remain confident in the industry-leading businesses that comprise our portfolio. We believe long-term value is driven by companies that can consistently grow earnings through various business cycles. We will continue to stay vigilant and invest in accordance with our investment philosophy, as we have done during previous periods of transformation.

data.title: KQQQ: Flexible Options Fund Providing Hefty Distribution Yield
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Kurv Technology Titans Select ETF (KQQQ) takes a unique approach to the covered call writing fund strategy. We've seen a number of newer exchange-traded funds pop up in the last several years that have taken otherwise more unique strategies to generate 'income.' With the Kurv fund sponsor, they've moved to be even a bit more unique relative to YieldMax, NEOS, and Roundhill funds. Often mixing in more flexibility to employ strategies rather than short calls alone. Though the other funds have been adopting more flexibility in their strategies to adapt to various situations, KQQQ seems to be more liberal in employing these additional "options."

The fund is also fairly uniquely positioned in that the overall exposure of the portfolio is not tied directly to an index, and they attempt to employ these options overlay strategies. Instead, they've actually taken the more "active" part of active ETF strategies and employed this on both the options and the underlying individual stock-picking side. That is, KQQQ invests in concentrated exposure to what they believe are "top technology growth stocks." That puts KQQQ once again in a bit of a differentiated strategy that makes it hard to necessarily define an exact peer group of funds.

KQQQ Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Distribution Yield: 17.24% (0.98% SEC yield)
  • Expense Ratio: 1.12%
  • Leverage: N/M
  • Managed Assets: $93.95 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

KQQQ's investment objective "seeks to maximize total return by actively managing a portfolio with concentrated exposure to high-conviction technology titans while, at the same time, generating potentially tax-efficient income." This comes in the form of looking for positioning that is weighted on "momentum signals" and "writing covered calls to generate income on a stock with limited upside potential or low price momentum, which can also serve as potential downside mitigation."

The fund is fairly small in terms of AUM, which I've noticed in other Kurv funds I've given a look at. They don't appear as popular as some of their more innovative peers. That said, relatively speaking, AUM has been on quite a surge in the last several months, so perhaps the group is starting to catch on.

In terms of leverage, the fund utilizes options, and that can end up where they are inherently leveraged, but they also leave the door open for leverage in the form of borrowings. At this time, they have a negative cash balance, but they hold significant exposure to risk-free U.S. Treasuries, and the negative balance at this point is negligible. That would indicate that they are not utilizing borrowings in a significant or meaningful manner.

Taking a Deeper Look

As this is our initial coverage, we'll take the time to dive a bit deeper into the investment strategy of the fund. To get that even deeper investment strategy, the prospectus expands from the key points presented on the website:

The Fund is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (“ETF”) that primarily invests its assets in equity securities of, or derivative instruments (e.g., options) relating to, individual U.S. and non-U.S. technology companies (“Technology Companies”) generally with market capitalizations in excess of $10 billion as well as the shares of other ETFs that invest in Technology Companies. The Fund defines “Technology Companies” to include those companies that provide technology products or services, that benefit from utilizing technology to gain competitive advantages, improve their business processes, products or applications, or that have introduced technologically enabled new products or services that potentially change the way the world works.

The Fund will primarily invest in the equity securities of, or derivative instruments (e.g., options) relating to, Technology Companies. The Fund will also invest in the Kurv Yield Premium ETFs (“Underlying Kurv Yield Premium ETFs”) and other Kurv ETFs (“Underlying Other Kurv ETFs” and, together with Underlying Kurv Yield Premium ETFs, the “Underlying Kurv ETFs”), which are ETFs advised by Kurv Investment Management LLC (the “Adviser”), the adviser to the Fund. A number of the Underlying Kurv ETFs have a primary investment objective to seek current income, and a secondary investment objective to seek exposure to the share price of the common stock (the “Underlying Security”) of a particular Technology Company (the “Underlying Issuer”), subject to a limit on potential investment gains. In selecting individual Technology Companies to invest in or have exposure to, the Adviser seeks Technology Companies with favorable outlooks, examining characteristics of a particular issuer, such as growth or momentum. The Fund may also invest in derivative instruments (e.g., options) of indices that includes Technology Companies.

With that, the fund ends up with a fairly concentrated portfolio, which is exactly what we should expect. On the other hand, we are still seeing 45 different positions—several of these are U.S. Treasury and option positions, though. Here is a look at the entire list of holdings as of January 13, 2026.

I'm sharing the long list to help illustrate and point out a couple of different strategies going on.

First, we can see at the top several different Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and NVIDIA (NVDA) options positions. These positions create a synthetic long position. We've seen this strategy employed in some funds on an index level, but with KQQQ, they are long call options, but then short put options. That creates a synthetic long position on these individual names where investors are exposed to upside participation, but some option premium is brought in because of selling the short puts. This is also the strategy being employed with Invesco QQQ (QQQ), which is notable as it means they'll implement ETFs within their portfolio as well.

That's where the following U.S. Treasury Bill exposure can come into play to create a sort of cash-secured short put trade. On the other hand, they would very likely roll the trades or close them rather than liquidate their Treasury Bills and take assignment.

The second observation here is that they are also long AAPL, MSFT, and NVDA shares directly, which can further create some potential upside, and they do not include any corresponding short call positions. That would mean that the upside exposure for those names is essentially uncapped; as the share price goes up, KQQQ participates in that upside completely. AAPL, MSFT, and NVDA are not the only names where we see this being the case; other names on the list are also held directly with no covered call position overlay. This simply tells us that the fund is not 100% overwritten, and that can lead to generally better performance during a rising market.

A third strategy we can also see is that the fund applies to your more traditional covered call situations. We can take a look at the Broadcom (AVGO) position, where they are long 18,535, but then have a short call position listed as well—noting that it is 90 contracts, meaning it is only about 50% overwritten. That also applies to names such as Meta Platforms (META) and Uber Technologies (UBER).

Finally, I'd also note that the fund has some puts they bought on the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS). This is a very small, nearly meaningless position in the grand scheme of things; however, it can at least show that they will include some more aggressive hedging techniques, even if ever so slightly currently.

To sum up, the manager will employ nearly any options strategy they see fit.

Performance Comparison

As we noted, KQQQ is fairly unique, so putting it up against some popular call writing peers, some of whom have employed various option strategies beyond short calls these days, can be quite difficult. For some context, though, we'll look at several of the popular Nasdaq Index-oriented funds, as the fund concentrates significantly in technology. That can include funds such as the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), and Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE).

Finally, I'll also include the Invesco QQQ (QQQ) and ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK)—QQQ being a representation of the straight Nasdaq 100 and ARKK being a collection of "innovation" growth companies.

With that, here are the total return comparisons between all of them since the inception of KQQQ, as it has the latest inception date of July 22, 2024. The next fund before that was QDTE, with an inception of March 7, 2024, and then QQQI on January 30, 2024, and then GPIQ, which came in October 2023.

Interestingly, the ARKK fund has blown away the results of all the rest, but with significantly greater volatility. The remainder all came in relatively similarly, with QQQI being the worst during this time period, but KQQQ only marginally better. However, we can also see that the fund was starting to outperform the rest around the September into November time period. To see this more clearly, we'll remove the outlier ARKK.

I would reiterate, though, that these funds were provided to show some relative context of more tech-oriented fund performance. There could be arguments to hold a couple or all of these funds at the same time. In fact, I hold QDTE and have QQQI on my "buy on the next broader market pullback list." KQQQ may be looking to join that same list after giving it a look today.

Hefty Distribution Rate

Based on the fund's latest information, the distribution rate works out to a significant 17.24%. The 30-Day SEC Yield comes to 0.98%, which is primarily going to be generated from the U.S. Treasury Bill/money market exposure of the fund. With that, we know that nearly all the payout is the result of the option premiums being generated from the call writing and the short put positions. This makes the payout quite significant, but it was more in the last handful of payouts (4-5) where the monthly distributions had been more outsized. Prior to that, we would have been in the 8-10% range.

What I can conclude (make a best guess based on observation) is that later in 2025, they shifted to being more aggressive with writing options. Whether this was because they wanted to be more defensive after such a solid run in overall equities or deliberately to generate a higher distribution rate is unclear. Whichever the case, the higher distribution also coincides with a significant increase in AUM...suggesting perhaps they noticed other fund sponsors gaining more interest based on higher distribution rates relative to their funds.

Either way, it will be interesting to see how this all plays out, and at this point, they seem to be very fluid with their overall strategy, making it a truly active fund. It'll be interesting to see how positioning changes in the future and if they'll be willing to change back aggressively to less overwriting in the right environment at the sacrifice of a lower distribution once again.

Conclusion

KQQQ employs a variety of options strategies while actively managing a concentrated underlying portfolio of exposure. This truly makes it an "actively" managed fund relative to some of what could be considered more mechanical approaches (i.e., invest in a Nasdaq-like portfolio and then utilize a call writing strategy to various degrees on that). The result so far has been, well, mixed.

At times, outperforming these counterparts but also seeing some weaker results at times. That isn't a negative necessarily against the fund, but an observation that the fund is differentiated. While we only have a relatively short track record at this point, it makes sense that the difference during this period has been fairly negligible. I would suspect that over the years, that is when we could start seeing more of a divergence.

The fund has seen a sizeable increase in its monthly distribution, which has increased the yield quite substantially now from where it had been. This would insinuate an increase in writing options to generate greater option premiums to pay out to investors. This could be the result of the overall broader market being a bit lofty here, a genuine strategy shift with management's outlook; alternatively, the more cynical side of me would be that it is designed to draw more attention to raise AUM.

With all that said, I will keep KQQQ on my watchlist as an interesting play to consider on a broader market pullback, which would really make me feel better overall in putting capital to work more aggressively.

data.title: HQH: Sentiment And Fundamentals Disconnect Presents Opportunity
data.description:
In the chart below, I have compared S&P500 (or SPY) against XBI, IBB and abrdn Healthcare Investors (HQH).

To understand the thesis, I am going to present here, look at the paths, not the endpoints alone. Throughout much of the year, biotech broadly underperformed the market. However, at some point in October, both IBB, representing commercial biotech behavior, and XBI, representing biotech sentiment extremes, moved ahead of SPY, ending with 25% and 40% gains respectively, while SPY gained 16%. HQH, by contrast, is up less than 10%.

This divergence is not a rejection of my thesis - it is the thesis. As uncertainty reduced, biotech indices repriced quickly, however HQH’s market price lagged due to its closed-end, discount-to-NAV structure. Healthcare CEFs tend to close this gap slower, as sentiment stabilizes. That gives rise to an attractive scenario, where its underlying asset class has recovered, but the CEF itself is dragging its feet.

So my HQH investment thesis consists of three parts. First, the healthcare sector itself is recovering as several long-standing sources of uncertainty begin to clear. Second, HQH is in a position to benefit from that recovery because of what it owns and how it is managed. Third, things have improved, however, for reasons that I will argue are wholly immaterial, HQH’s market price has not recovered - yet. This creates an opportunity.

Quick Overview Of HQH

HQH is a CEF that invests in healthcare stocks with strong balance sheets. The idea is to avoid “science projects” or binary events, and focus on steady, “boring” long-term capital appreciation. HQH is not looking for huge home runs - it is aiming to get there slowly but steadily.

Current AUM is ~$1.2 billion, expense ratio is ~1.1%, and HQH is trading at a ~8% discount to NAV. HQH comes with a managed distribution policy, paying quarterly distributions, mainly from realized gains, with nearly nothing from net investment income.

66.8% of the portfolio was in biotechnology in September, followed by 10.9% in pharmaceuticals, and then small allocations in healthcare equipment, life-sciences tools, and services. Total holdings are in some 140–150 positions, and the top 10 accounted for ~23.4% of the portfolio by December. Top holdings include uniQure (QURE) at 3.06%, Amgen (AMGN) at 3.36% and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) at 1.29%. Most are either late-stage or commercial stage companies. The emphasis is on companies with late-stage pipelines, well-funded balance sheets, and visible regulatory or commercial catalysts.

Biotech Recovering From Medicare Uncertainty

In the last several years, Medicare drug pricing remained an “un-modelable” quantity. The Inflation Reduction Act was passed in 2022, but three unresolved uncertainties remained: a) Which drugs would actually be affected, b) How big the price cuts would really be, and c) How predictable the rules would be in practice. This uncertainty hit healthcare equities from all sides - even if they were selling/pipelining drugs that weren’t directly exposed to Medicare.

In 2026, some of the new Medicare-negotiated drug prices will come into effect. At least a part of the list of drugs will be made clear, and pricing will be made explicit. Investors can now use these numbers to assess cash flow and return. The risk, that was open-ended earlier, will be gone. This will reduce much of the uncertainty surrounding Medicare.

This matters directly to HQH’s portfolio composition. Most of these are large, multi-asset companies that can make money in multiple ways. If Medicare lowers prices, they can sell more volumes (volume may rise as price goes down), move to other drugs, extend the drug’s market life, or acquire other assets. They don’t actually depend on Medicare - they just need to get Medicare to remove that uncertainty that’s been killing the entire sector.

From a market perspective, as well, HQH does not need Medicare to not lower prices. It just needs the market to stop treating Medicare as an unknowable political slugfest. Once that happens, the valuation discount that’s being applied to the sector as a whole will recover.

HQH's Portfolio And Management Strategy

HQH’s portfolio folds in mostly commercial stage companies, or those with a clear path forward towards commercialization. As of December, there were 146 positions, with the top 10 accounting for roughly 23% of NAV and the largest single holding representing just over 3%. So this sort of diversification prevents any one name from overwhelming the portfolio.

15% of the portfolio is invested in established healthcare companies such as Amgen, Regeneron, Abbott, Danaher, Humana, and Merck. These are large companies with approved products, recurring revenue, and the ability to fund pipelines internally. Another ~20% is allocated to late-stage biotech with Phase 3 assets, marketed products, or clear regulatory pathways, including uniQure, argenx, Insmed, Ultragenyx, Sarepta, Cytokinetics, and Vaxcyte.

In the current constricted market, this positioning is useful. With capital discipline making a comeback, early-stage biotech isn’t getting a lot of funds, and open market dilutive funding is getting difficult. This is where late-stage and commercial assets benefit from licensing activity, partnerships, and acquisitions. HQH owns a broad enough set of M&A-ready assets across indications and modalities, so it benefits from portfolio-level activity rather than single name events.

A Quick Look At The High Yield And Its Sources

As of December 31, 2025, HQH’s forward annual distribution is $2.28 per share, paid quarterly at $0.57 per share, implying a market yield of ~11.7–12.4%. According to HQH’s most recent Section 19 notice, the entire distribution came from realized gains, with nothing from return of capital and nothing from net investment income. Of realized gains, 51% came from short-term gains and 49% from long-term gains.

Therefore, it is meaningless to approach HQH with payout ratios and dividend coverage metrics. Since there is no underlying income, such metrics don’t work here. HQH only pays through realized gains. Distribution is only driven by trading activity and NAV behavior.

HQH has historically done well with this approach. In the last five years, average annual total return on NAV was 9.20%. Besides, they were also able to maintain a strong dividend through successful trading. HQH did not need to erode its balance sheet to support distribution. Its current NAV distribution rate of ~11.4% is also higher than its five-year NAV return. That shows that distributions can exceed annual NAV growth in strong years.

NAV, Discount, And Why Timing Matters

Despite all that, HQH is trading at a ~8% discount to NAV, meaning investors can buy HQH for around $0.92 on the dollar. This discount persists despite its five-year run rate. In other words, the fund’s performance has been significantly stronger than what the market sentiment would have us believe.

This disconnect comes directly from the uncertainty in the sector, as it affects healthcare-focused closed-end funds differently than healthcare equities. What happens is, once sentiment improves, individual equities and their indices improve. A CEF’s NAV also responds to the improving sentiment, portfolio fundamentals and realized gains. However, market price tends to lag because closed-end fund prices respond to investor confidence in the vehicle, not just improvement in the underlying assets. In healthcare that confidence returns only after policy risk and distribution concerns are seen as resolved, so NAV recovers first while market price lags.

The recovery math does not need aggressive assumptions. If NAV simply keeps steady and the discount merely returns to par, investors buying in today capture an incremental 8-9% return. That’s on top of whatever portfolio performance there might be. Important to understand, this setup does not need a major biotech boom, or buying early stage binary candidates. This comes simply from realized gains from steady late-stage commercial assets added to the discount compression.

The bottom-line to understand is - since the underlying asset class has recovered, there is no fundamental reason HQH should not recover as well. It just takes more time - and there lies the opportunity.

What Would Break The Thesis

Several risks exist that could disrupt the opportunity. First, we have the new Medicare policy in the abstract, but it needs to be properly implemented. If there are surprises in its scope or enforcement, that could reintroduce the same uncertainty we have been afraid of - and hurt my buy thesis.

A second risk is that even if the sector keeps improving, investor confidence may not return anytime soon. A CEF has a complex structure and distribution/return mechanism compared to equities - that is obvious - so it may just take too much time for a proper assessment that would revive confidence.

Even if all else goes well, HQH’s distribution has no steady income backing it, just realized gains. When markets are flat, or when monetizable catalytic events do not exist, payouts may fluctuate.

A final risk I need to cover is the inherent flaky nature of the healthcare sector. It is a good sector for asymmetric opportunities, but it is not a dependable sector. So it is entirely possible that sentiment will deteriorate once again despite improved fundamentals. If that happens, HQH’s NAV recovery would stall.

Conclusion

HQH has solid fundamentals and a strong distribution. HQH, along with the rest of the healthcare sector, was down for most of the last few years. The broad sector, beginning around last Fall, has been recovering. That means, the underlying asset class of HQH is recovering.

However, HQH is still trading at a nice discount to NAV. I have argued here why it is doing that, and why it should recover. If I am correct, this is a good opportunity to enter into a solid income investment and effectively buy a recovered asset class through a vehicle that has not yet caught up. This disconnect presents the opportunity.

data.title: The Tide Is Lifting Giants, Leaving Varonis Behind
data.description:
Thesis

Varonis Systems, Inc. (VRNS), is a cybersecurity software company that offers a data security platform. As you'd imagine, its products help companies find and organize sensitive data, protect it from misuse, detect security threats, and automatically fix issues when they arise.

Over the past year, VRNS has significantly underperformed the broader market (SP500). Most likely, considering where the trend is headed, I’m bearish on owning the shares as expanding cloud migrations keep squeezing smaller cybersecurity vendors like Varonis in a market that’s clearly consolidating.

When the Tide Doesn’t Lift All Boats

The cloud's supposed to be a rising tide for cybersecurity. But for Varonis Systems, it’s turning into a headwind. As more computing shifts to platforms controlled by a few giants, standalone firms that don’t own the infrastructure are getting squeezed instead of lifted. As enterprises rush faster into cloud-first architectures, budgets are consolidating. And that’s the problem.

You might have seen Morgan Stanley’s note a couple of days ago about how around 47% of workloads now live in the cloud, nearly doubling from just a few years ago. Not really a shock for an investor who even casually follows this particular neck of the woods. Naturally, connecting the dots leads to the conclusion that as this sharp shift accelerates, it’s becoming much easier to see who is really coming out ahead.

One clear example that Morgan Stanley points out is the hyperscalers, such as Microsoft (MSFT). This portfolio must-have, IMO, now controls roughly 14% of the global cybersecurity software market. Five years ago, the number was roughly a third of what it is now. So what’s notable is that they’re still running essentially the same platforms. The difference, again, no surprise, is scale. They’re much bigger, and that size gives them far more weight.

Google (GOOG) is another obvious example of how seriously this effort's being taken now. Time flies, but I'm sure you'll recall its recent deal to gobble up cloud security startup Wiz for around $32 billion, expected to close this year, which would rank among the largest cybersecurity deals ever, at least for now. So the message is definitely hard to miss. Security is a native feature of the cloud platforms themselves, just as natural as having a bolt lock on your front door.

Nevertheless, it's usually the small guy who has the hardest time keeping up. For vendors that aren't big enough to bundle their products into large cloud ecosystems, they're seeing a clear and growing loss of customer spending. And Varonis isn't immune. I'm not implying that its focus on data security posture management isn't strong; it's really about the way customers, in particular those who give the thumbs-up buying decisions around these products, have changed. CISOs (chief information security officers) aren’t shopping in a vacuum anymore.

Simple is better, and less is more, I say. By next year, Gartner expects nearly 70% of organizations to cap their cloud-native application vendors at three or fewer. Once that limit is in place, we'll see more vendors suddenly competing for survival, not just growth. Microsoft's got a slight edge right now. Admittedly, it's not a deep dive; still, reviews on G2 show Microsoft Defender for Cloud scoring a bit higher on ease of use and setup than Varonis Systems’ DSPM offering.

Losing Ground in the Cloud Era

At its appearance at the Barclays conference late last year, management said SaaS is now a split business. On one side, SaaS is doing what it’s supposed to do. New business is coming in, and upsells are working.

On the other side? Unfortunately, the legacy base is unraveling faster than expected. On-prem subscription renewals fell well below expectations in the most recent quarter, driven largely by single-use customers who didn’t stick around. And there you have it, the danger zone in any transition. The old engine's slowing down, while the new one still isn’t big enough to fully take over yet.

Renewal rates improved earlier in the year, but closer review at the end of the quarter and mistakes in how sales were handled led to higher customer losses. This outweighed the progress the company made toward reaching 76% SaaS annual recurring revenue. I think it's also notable that federal deals struggled. Part of this was due to the FedRAMP certification being completed in May, which interrupted active discussions and pushed deal timelines out in a quarter where most sales were expected to close late.

Meanwhile, Morgan Stanley's not the only one waving yellow caution flags. A couple of weeks ago, Citi’s negative catalyst watch also pointed to rising competition in data security. It warned that net new annual recurring revenue could be uneven this year as competitors gain ground and potential buyers hold back because of churn risk.

By contrast, CrowdStrike (CRWD) is doing well in the same market. Its cloud-native architecture allows it to benefit from current customer buying patterns instead of being hurt by them. In its recent earnings, it reported ending annual recurring revenue of $4.92 billion, up 23% year-over-year, punctuated by growth that came from AI-driven demand. It pushed customers to adopt more modules and, as a result, helped keep momentum strong around that single-agent platform trend. CrowdStrike, meanwhile, posted a 73% jump in net new annual recurring revenue in the same quarter. It’s another example of how scale pays off, letting it capture bundled spending that a narrower vendor like Varonis struggles to reach.

Looking ahead, the key point is that this cloud-related pressure could cap Varonis Systems’ upside over the next few quarters unless its move to SaaS grows faster than the market share it’s losing. If that doesn’t happen, ARR growth may remain in the mid-teens while competitors like CrowdStrike continue to grow faster. That would leave Varonis with limited options other than pushing harder on customer conversions or forming partnerships to relieve the pressure.

Hope is Not in the Multiple

VRNS is at about 5.6× forward EV-to-sales. That’s a meaningful premium, roughly 57% higher than the sector median of 3.5×. But it’s still about 36% below its own five-year average of 8.7. It’s not cheap versus peers, on one hand. Flip the coin, and we can see that the market's clearly marking it down versus its history, which tells you investors have been uneasy about how this SaaS transition is playing out.

Peers like CRWD trade at about 24.19× forward EV-to-sales, supported by roughly 23% ARR growth and an integrated platform.

PANW trades around 11.79×, benefiting from its larger scale, which will help protect it from hyperscalers moving into its space.

That slightly lower multiple's really just a risk discount. And here, the first risk showing up is execution, specifically through faster-than-expected churn in the legacy customer base. The second issue is the quality of growth. On top of that, the competitive environment is getting tougher.

Still, cash flow's positive. The stock trades around 34x forward price to cash flow, and consensus has EPS rebounding about 169% this year. That said, those swings also point to real volatility, which could make the results hard to sustain.

Final Verdict

Rating: Sell. Right now, there are a lot more quality ways to invest in this slice of the market, and I think, for VRNS, the downside risks simply outweigh the upside you can capture elsewhere. For those speculative traders out there, give it a go if the bet's based on a rapid SaaS inflection.

data.title: Insiders Bought The Dip, Now Nasdaq Eyes All-Time Highs
data.description:
The Market Calmly Ignores The Greenland Jitters

It was only barely a week ago that President Donald Trump stunned the world with his Greenland bluster, which threatened to send stocks into a downward spiral, although that pessimism never really took hold. Dip buyers returned confidently to ignore these geopolitically-linked upheavals (read: opportunities), betting that some form of détente will return eventually to help calm the markets once more. And guess what? While the health insurers suffered a bruising Tuesday, as the pain from a "near-flat Medicare rate increase" for 2027 added onto the weak performance from UnitedHealth's Q4 2025 release and guidance (UNH), the tech-heavy Nasdaq (QQQ) (NDX) has recovered all the losses from last week, and gained further ground.

As a result, it's all systems green again for growth and tech investors who ignored the noise from last week's downside volatility to add to their positions. I've been alerting such an opportunity to subscribers in my Investing Group: Ultimate Growth Investing to get their dry powder and take the opportunity to accumulate through an update on January 22, when the world was still awash with the media narrative that warns the world about the changing order that is about to unfold in front of our eyes:

As I highlighted in yesterday evening's quick chat update, we have managed to retest (1st condition), and also bounce off (2nd condition) off the 50-period moving average (blue line). I've enunciated these are key milestones as the Nasdaq approached the inflection point this week, as investors attempted to price in a very hawkish Greenland headwind. Nevertheless, we've also learned to never fall into the trappings of geopolitical headwinds by simply reading from the headlines of the media, who are obviously looking for the clicks and baits. Rather, we focus on what truly matters, and that's often printed in the market tape. And the tape shows that buyers aren't prepared to give up that 50-period MA yet, and will thus attempt another go to break the December highs that I also marked in the chart. - Exclusive market update to subscribers of Ultimate Growth Investing on January 22

Growth And Tech Investors Getting All Fired Up

And that inflection point has worked seamlessly, and almost to perfection, drawing in buyers looking for an advancing opportunity, while the world worries about what's next for America and its allies. And as things stand now in pre-market trading on the Nasdaq futures (+0.8%), we are laying the path to retake 2025's highs, and stage what I believe will be a decisive breakout for 2026.

Fears about a reallocation away from growth and tech have threatened to upend the tech and growth stock leadership. However, the narrative hasn't quite caught up with the underlying market action (I always believe actions count more than words).

There are several ways to analyze these "actions," depending on your investment strategy. For me, besides trying to look into the "tea leaves" through price action analysis, I also want to know what the insiders are doing under the hood, beyond what they are telling the wider financial media at Davos. Why? Because if they think that the current market regime is still highly constructive for the market's next potential advancing phase, then we should see insiders loading up, right? Does it make any sense to you?

Nasdaq Insiders Confidently Load Up

And that's precisely what I've observed. Nasdaq insiders are using pullbacks to accumulate, even as the market marks new highs. And, as the market has consolidated from Q4 of 2025, the insider buy signals have only gotten stronger relative to the sell impulses, as seen in the upward surge in the Nasdaq Buy/Sell ratio. Therefore, it makes little sense for me to turn against the tech people and "geniuses" who I believe know "best" about their businesses, and while the rest of the market unduly worries about whether the so-called AI bubble can still persist, or is it close to an imminent implosion?

And as if the upgraded CapEx from TSMC (TSM) earlier in January wasn't enough to convince the market that we are far from seeing a bubble burst, European-based advanced lithography systems supplier, ASML (ASML), has just unleashed a set of incredible results that surpassed Wall Street's outlook. Note that ASML posted net bookings of €13.2B, as compared to the €6.85B penned in by analysts. Such clarity in ASML's bookings should assure doubters about the strength of the AI boom through the end of the decade. I believe the market has finally gotten the impetus it requires for buyers to confidently load up while ignoring the bearish prognosticators, and help reverse the previous market rotation to value stocks and back into the arms of the tech leaders.

Growth Versus Value At An Inflection Point

We can also glean such signals that demonstrate my thesis since the bottom last week, when overlaying the price action seen in growth (VUG) over value (VTV). Note that the multi-month correction for growth against value reversed into a blue candlestick last week. That wasn't an ordinary reversal, as it occurred right at the 50-week moving average (blue line) which has been a crucial dynamic support for the VUG/VTV uptrend bias for the past few years.

In other words, holding on to this line shows that the growth's narrative stays resilient and remains relevant. In contrast, losing this support may even trigger a worse selloff, as investors closely watching for patterns of dip buying consider abandoning their conviction, worsening subsequent pullbacks and drawdown phases.

Thankfully, this downcast stance is about to meet its moment of reckoning, while there's also a bear trap (false downside breakdown) that appears to be bottoming out over the support level from August 2025.

So, if you had wanted a clearer signal to move back into growth and tech, then the time is now. Just don't wait until VUG/VTV reclaims the all time highs before you get that buy trigger going.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Starrygazey Aims For IPO On Excessive Valuation, Little Revenue
data.description:
Starrygazey Has Grown, but Valuation at IPO is Excessive

Starrygazey (MARH) has filed to raise growth capital in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

MARH provides a variety of business services for IPO candidate companies in Hong Kong.

The firm faces numerous risks, has a short operating history, has high client concentration, and its valuation assumptions are excessive.

My outlook on the MARH IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Starrygazey Do?

Hong Kong, China-based Starrygazey was founded to develop a range of services to advise companies preparing to go public in Hong Kong and for post-IPO consulting.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mr. Ka Chun (Matthew) Wong, who has been with the firm since September 2025 and was previously an auditor at Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu and PricewaterhouseCoopers. He's currently a vice president of Ample Capital Limited.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Pre-IPO advisory services

  • IPO advisory

  • Post-IPO advisory

  • ESG consulting services

MARH makes money primarily on a contractual basis for individual client engagements for its services.

Starrygazey has booked a fair market value investment of $1 through June 30, 2025, from investors, including MARH Limited, Applied Equity International, Major Treasure International, and Ruyi Strategic Investments.

The company was originally capitalized by loans from related parties, which have since been repaid.

What's Starrygazey’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by HK TDC, the Hong Kong market for financial services is a substantial economic pillar of the region and accounted for more than 26% of the city's GDP in 2024.

The city is regularly ranked in the top five globally for financial centers in terms of market capitalization and has an active market for new listings.

By November 2025, the Hong Kong market hosted 93 IPOs, with gross proceeds of HK$259 billion ($33.7 billion).

Also, the total market capitalization of the 2,352 listed companies in November 2025 was HK$47.9 trillion ($6.2 trillion).

The company operates in an intensely competitive industry that features a wide variety of small, medium, and large financial advisory firms with deep pockets and entrenched relationships.

The firm targets its services at small and medium-sized businesses in the Hong Kong-Guangdong-Macau region (Greater Bay Area) of China.

It focuses on providing one-stop solutions in accounting, finance, and pre-IPO advisory practice areas.

MARH has filed to sell 3.75 million shares of Class A ordinary shares at a proposed midpoint price of $4.50 per share for gross proceeds of approximately $16.875 million, excluding the sale of underwriter options.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

The Class B shareholder will receive 20 votes per share and will have voting control of the company immediately post-IPO.

Company, shareholder (greater than 5%), and executive lock-ups will be for the typical 180 days after the effective date of the IPO prospectus.

According to Starrygazey’s most recent regulatory filing, it says it will use the net proceeds as described here:

Brand promotion and marketing - 20%

Recruitment of talented personnel - 20%

Expansion to new offices and expanding the scope of services that we provide - 50%

General working capital - 10%

(Source - SEC)

The company’s market capitalization at IPO (ex-underwriter options) will approximate $109 million.

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio will be approximately 15.5% (excluding underwriter options).

The firm’s presentation for its roadshow is not available.

As to its possible legal exposures, management said the firm is not presently a party to "any material legal or administrative proceedings."

The only listed underwriter of the IPO is Pacific Century Securities.

Starrygazey is Growing, but Risks Are Many

MARH is seeking US public capital market investment to expand its operations.

The company’s financials have produced increasing topline revenue from a small base, a swing to gross profit and operating profit, and growing cash flow from operations.

General & Administrative expenses as a function of total revenue rose as revenue increased, and its General & Administrative efficiency multiple was 92.5x.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will retain all available funds for its growth and working capital requirements.

MARH’s recent capital spending history shows it hasn’t spent on capital expenditures at all in the trailing 12-month period.

Related party transactions are shown in detail here.

The firm’s growth strategy includes growing its corporate consulting and ESG service capabilities, expanding beyond the Hong Kong region to other Asian markets, and investing in complementary business ventures to diversify its revenue base.

The market opportunity for providing IPO-related services in Hong Kong is moderate and subject to market volatility in recent years due to political and social instability.

Risks to the company include its "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company" status, which enables management to produce substantially less information for investors.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO or with high volatility in public markets.

The firm has geographic and customer concentration risks for its revenue sources.

As to valuation, Starrygazey is asking IPO investors to pay far higher valuation multiples than a basket of leading public financial service firms in the US, despite having a small book of business, a short operating history, and few employees.

It faces a wide variety of regulatory, compliance, and listing risks by virtue of its location and operations in Hong Kong while seeking to obtain a US listing.

Given the many risks it faces, its excessive valuation assumptions, and its small size, my outlook on the MARH IPO is to sell (avoid it).

data.description:
Key takeaways

New portfolio positioning in place

As announced on June 23, 2025, Invesco Developing Markets Fund transitioned to new leadership under the Asian and Emerging Markets Equities Investment Team, which resulted in a change to the fund’s investment philosophy and process.

The fund outperformed its benchmark

Invesco Developing Markets Fund Class A (ODMAX) shares at net asset value outperformed the MSCI EM Index in the fourth quarter of 2025. Stock selection in consumer discretionary was the largest contributor to relative return.

Emerging markets performance

Emerging market equities delivered a positive return in the fourth quarter, outperforming US equities and underperforming developed international equities. 1

Investment objective

The fund seeks long-term growth of capital.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M)

9,131.63

Portfolio managers

Charles Bond, Ian Hargreaves, Matthew Pigott, William Lam

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Emerging market equities had another positive quarter, outperformed US equities for the third consecutive quarter, and underperformed developed international equities. 1
  • For 2025, the MSCI EM Index returned 33.57% compared to 31.89% and 17.88% for the MSCI EAFE and S&P 500 Indexes, respectively.
  • South Korea posted the highest return for the quarter, with strong contributions from technology stocks like SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics. Latin America, along with Taiwan and South Africa, also outperformed the index for the quarter. China and Saudi Arabia were key underperformers.
  • We believe emerging market equities offer double-digit earnings growth potential, with reasonable valuations across much of the universe. Despite recent outperformance, the asset class has been trading at a discount to global equities, particularly the US. Easier US monetary policy could be a tailwind for emerging markets, particularly given a weakening US dollar.
  • While we are mindful of geopolitical risks and uncertainty tied to evolving trade policy, emerging market corporates generally appear to have healthy balance sheets and competitive advantages that could make them more resilient than what is implied in valuations. Moreover, if specific global trade channels are forced to reconfigure away from China, other emerging countries could benefit, fostering further growth in trade among emerging countries.

1 Emerging market equities are represented by the MSCI Emerging Markets (EM) Index which returned 4.73% in Q4 2025. International developed stocks are represented by the MSCI EAFE Index (The MSCI EAFE Index is an equity index which captures large and mid cap representation across 21 Developed Markets countries* around the world, excluding the US and Canada) which returned 4.91% in Q4 2025. US equities are represented by the S&P 500 which returned 2.66% in Q4 2025.

Performance highlights

From a sector perspective, stock selection in consumer discretionary, consumer staples and IT added to relative results. The largest detractor from relative return was communication services due to stock selection and an overweight, followed by selection in energy.

Geographically, stock selection in India, stock selection in Thailand and an underweight in Saudi Arabia added to relative return. The largest detractor from relative return was an overweight in Singapore. Underweights in South Korea and South Africa also detracted.

Contributors to absolute performance

Samsung Electronics is a leading manufacturer of consumer and industrial electronic equipment. Earnings estimates for Samsung rose on the belief that ongoing demand for AI infrastructure would drive prices for its memory chips. We have always liked Samsung's strong balance sheet and robust free cash flow, particularly because shareholder return appears to be near the top of the company’s agenda.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is one of the world's leading semiconductor foundries and a key enabler of the new computing revolution. It is known for advanced technology, high volume production and a robust customer base, including major technology companies like Apple, Qualcomm and NVIDIA (not fund holdings).

Vale, a Brazilian metal mining company and one of the world’s largest iron ore producers, also produces nickel and copper. The stock had underperformed iron ore peers over the past three years as it emerged from a challenging operational period. With low expectations across key segments, there is scope for the stock’s further recovery. Iron ore remains central to our investment case and we believe industry returns are likely to be resilient over the medium term.

Detractors from absolute return

Tencent, one of China’s leading internet companies, has strong positions in gaming, social media, digital content and cloud services. It has been investing heavily in AI infrastructure that has been integrated across its platforms. As of September 30, 2025, the stock had a 60% year-to-date return. In the fourth quarter, profit-taking, weaker-than-expected economic data and China’s ongoing policy uncertainty appeared to weigh on the stock.

Alibaba is one of the world’s largest e-commerce and retail platforms. Over the past year, the company has undergone a strategic reorganization, including divestment of non-core assets, stabilization of its e-commerce market share and improvements to its organizational structure.

JD.com is China’s second largest e-commerce company. Weakening sales trends in certain categories, in some cases relative to strong previous sales, appeared to weigh on the stock. Also, management’s strategic focus on the highly competitive food delivery industry appeared to raise some concerns about capital allocation decisions.

Growth stocks tend to be more sensitive to changes in their earnings and can be more volatile.

The Fund may hold illiquid securities that it may be unable to sell at the preferred time or price and could lose its entire investment in such securities.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

Investing in securities of Chinese companies involves additional risks, including, but not limited to: the economy of China differs, often unfavorably, from the U.S. economy in such respects as structure, general development, government involvement, wealth distribution, rate of inflation, growth rate, allocation of resources and capital reinvestment, among others; the central government has historically exercised substantial control over virtually every sector of the Chinese economy through administrative regulation and/or state ownership; and actions of the Chinese central and local government authorities continue to have a substantial effect on economic conditions in China.

* Alpha (cash adjusted) is a measure of performance on a risk-adjusted basis. Beta (cash adjusted) is a measure of relative risk and the slope of regression. Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earning per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund’s financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both

data.title: South Plains Financial: A Solid Bank At A Fair Valuation (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of South Plains Financial (SPFI) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 10%. South Plains has been working aggressively to grow in its key Texas markets as it seeks to build scale, and in December, it announced a deal to buy BOH Holdings for $106 million in stock. The Texas market has been a key focus for industry growth and consolidation, and as I expected, SPFI aims to be a buyer rather than a seller.

I last covered shares in July when I rated South Plains a “Sell,” and since then, the stock has lost 1%, missing out on a 10% market rally. With updated financials and an M&A transaction, now is a good time to revisit SPFI.

BOH purchase highlights a solid Q4

In the company’s fourth quarter, South Plains earned $0.90 per share, which beat expectations by $0.05 as revenue grew 4% to $54 million. For the full year, the company earned $3.44. Back in July, I saw the company with about $3.20 in earnings power. Stronger loan growth and better margins enabled results to come in a bit better than I expected. The company continues to perform well, but the big news during the quarter was its M&A announcement.

As you can see below, South Plains operates in more rural parts of Texas, around its Lubbock hub, though it also has been expanding into Dallas and El Paso to capitalize on faster growth in these metros. The BOH acquisition will give it a better footprint in Houston, one of the fastest-growing metros in America. Still with only two branches in Houston, SPFI will not be among the 10 biggest Texas-based banks in Houston. While SPFI is adding scale, its Dallas & Houston operations are nowhere near as scaled as its Lubbock base, and it will take significant investment and strong execution over several years to do this. With $5.4 billion of combined assets, SPFI remains well below the $10 billion threshold to graduate from community to regional bank, and I would not be surprised to see it pursue further deals to add scale and support its growth ambitions. While the company said it is not a “serial” acquirer, it said it would not “be afraid” of doing additional deals.

Turning to its own results, deposits ended the quarter at $3.87 billion. Deposit costs fell by 9bps sequentially to 2.01%. Total deposits are up about $250 million from last year, a strong pace of growth, and I have been encouraged by the year-over-year growth in noninterest-bearing balances, which are over 26% of deposits. I expect this share to be stable over the next year. SPFI maintains a granular, low-cost deposit base. It will be critical for SPFI to use its deposit-gathering skills towards BOH, which has been reliant on higher-cost jumbo deposits.

Loans were up 2.9% from last year at $3.1 billion. The average loan yield fell by 13bps to 6.79%, given the impact of Fed rate cuts on floating-rate loans. As with most community banks, its loan portfolio is geared towards commercial real estate (“CRE”) with a 37% allocation to the sector. Multifamily exposure is its largest at 20%, and just 12% of the CRE portfolio is in office. The portfolio has a 55% loan-to-value, providing a very strong equity cushion. This conservative underwriting approach has helped to deliver excellent underwriting results.

Beyond this, the company also has a $241 million indirect auto loan portfolio. This portfolio is quite high quality with 68% allocated to super prime borrowers (FICO over 719), and its delinquency rate is just 19bps. For perspective, Ally’s (ALLY) delinquency is over 4%, and Ally is a solid underwriter. Now, I would note the portfolio has seen some credit migration. While just 6% were below prime at origination, about 12% are now with 3.8% deeply subprime. As such, I would not be surprised to see delinquencies rise this year, though the ultimate risk of lifetime losses is almost certainly below $10 million, a very manageable sum for SPFI.

South Plains had a 4.00% net interest margin (“NIM”) in the quarter, up 25bps from last year thanks to lower funding costs. NIM was down 5bps sequentially, but Q3 was boosted by receiving back interest on a positive credit workout on a loan previously moved into nonaccrual status. Thus far, SPFI has been able to offset Fed rate cuts with lower deposit costs, and I expect NIM to be in the 3.9%-4% range in 2026.

Now, I would note that SPFI should generate about $15 million of interchange fees this year. Across the sector, I have highlighted these fees as being at risk if proposed legislative action occurs to reduce swipe fees. I see $3-5% million of revenue being at risk, or about $0.15-$0.20 per share, a potential 5% hit to earnings. Passage is highly uncertain, and this risk may not come to fruition, but investors should be mindful of it as SPFI has higher exposure than many banks.

SPFI has strong reserves and capital

South Plains has excellent credit quality. Charge-offs were a modest 10bps. During the quarter, SPFI set aside $1.8 million for credit losses, primarily due to ongoing loan growth. Allowances now cover 1.44% of loans. Nonperforming assets were flat at 26bps, and during 2026, there were several loans that recovered from nonaccrual status. Allowances provide more than 4x coverage of NPLs. I view coverage above 2.5x as excellent. With its excess reserve position, SPFI is well positioned for a material degradation of credit quality before having to build reserves further.

Beyond strong reserves, South Plains also has a strong balance sheet. Its tier 1 common equity (“CET1”) capital ratio is an excellent 14.45%, up nearly 100bps from a year ago. Even including its $48 million unrealized loss in AOCI, capital would be over 13%, a strong level. Given its size, these losses will never need to be included in its capital calculations. Earlier this month, South Plains raised its dividend by 6%, and shares now offer a 1.7% dividend yield. I view its dividend as highly secure given its capital position.

Shares are a hold with a ~10x pro forma multiple

Looking forward, management is targeting an acceleration in loan growth to mid-to-high single digits in 2026, which I view as attainable. I expect industrywide loan growth to improve this year given lower borrowing costs and reduced macro uncertainty. As such, SPFI should be able to improve on its ~3% loan growth in 2025, though I expect loan growth this year to be closer to 5% than 7% as multifamily repayments reduce Q1 loan balances temporarily. I also expect NIM to be only modestly below 2025 levels given deposit pricing power, and credit costs should be similar to 2025.

Given these factors, I am looking for SPFI to earn $3.65-$3.85 in 2026. This leaves shares about 10.8x earnings, which is a bit expensive, as community banks generally struggle to trade much past 10x, given their small size and lack of index inclusion. The BOH deal should eventually add at least $0.30 of EPS, assuming successful improvement of its deposit base, leaving its pro-forma multiple closer to 10x. Tangible book value is $29, and shares are about 1.4x book value, which I view as reasonable. M&A has been occurring at over 1.7x book, so the market is appropriately pricing in that SPFI is unlikely to be a seller.

At about 10x earnings, SPFI is not overly expensive, and its TX location is likely to keep a bid in shares given ongoing consolidation. As such, I do not see shares as a “sell” after their relative underperformance of the past six months. That said, valuation already reflects SPFI’s own M&A benefits, and I see multiple expansion. As such, I view shares as a “Hold.” Within the sector, I prefer a stock like Horizon (HBNC), has a clear earnings growth story at a lower multiple. At 10x already, SPFI is not compelling enough to merit a “Buy” but is a suitable hold in portfolios.
